coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots embedded silver sulfide molybdenum disulfide for efficient electrode materials for hybrid supercapacitors and electrocatalytic hydrogen generation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Abbas, Tasawar
Umar, Ehtisham
Hassan, Haseebul
Iqbal, M. Waqas
Ullah, Hameed
Khan, Sherdil
Mohammad, Saikh
Alotaibi, Nouf H.
Ismayilova, N.A.
Mohammad, Akbar
Alawaideh, Yazen.M.
coredata.dc:description: Development of sustainable and renewable energy sources, such as the water electro-reduction to produce hydrogen and various gadgets for storing energy, particularly increased power density and environmentally amiable supercapacitors, have grown necessary for energy security in light of current environmental concerns. Herein, silver sulfide (Ag2S) and molybdenum sulfide (MoS2) doped (10 wt%) with the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been created and investigated for use in electrochemical processes. In alkaline pH, the Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs heterostructure that was designed had a stable and improved HER (η = 98 mV, Tafel slope: 76 mV/dec) for up to 1000 CV cycles. This was due to its effective interface construction. Due to its exceptional storage capabilities, the asymmetric Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs//AC device also demonstrated good cycling stability (with 92.17 % of the capacitance remaining after 5000 cycles), an increased energy density (71 Wh/kg) and power density (850 W/kg) at 2 A/g), and an exceptional high specific capacity (500C/g at mass standardized current thickness of 2 A/g). Ag2S-MoS2 and rGO interact because of charge transfer from the Ag and Mo 3d orbitals to the C 2p orbitals. Because of this trade, the quantity of states per unit for hybrid structures exhibits amplification close to the Fermi level, indicating a rise in the material's conductivity. Additionally, the ternary heterostructure Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs has the largest quantum capacitance, which supports this system's experimentally determined higher charge storage ability. Technological perceptions on the creation of a cost-effective and efficient catalyst for flexible energy storage applications and sustainable hydrogen production are provided by this work.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs
Hydrogen evolution reaction
Supercapacitor
Energy and power density

The authors are grateful to Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Cient\u00EDfico e Tecnol\u00F3gico (CNPq) (Process: 306871/2021-1) and Funda\u00E7\u00E3o de Amparo \u00E0 Pesquisa do Estado do Rio Grande do Sul (FAPERGS) (Process: 24/2551-0001550-7).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of synthesis of Ag2S and MoS2.
(a) XRD pattern (b) UV–Vis spectra and (c) TGA analysis for Ag2S, MoS2 and Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs.
SEM images of (a) Ag2S, (b) MoS2, (c) Ag2S-MoS2 and (d) Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs composite.
XPS spectra of (a) Ag 3d, (b) Mo 3d, (c) S 2p, (d) C 1s, and (e) XPS spectra of the Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs composite.
(a) EIS analysis for Ag2S-MoS2 and Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs. (b–c) BET analysis of Ag2S-MoS2 and Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs, respectively.
CV graphs of (a) Ag2S, (b) MoS2, (c) Ag2S-MoS2 and (d) Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs at 3 to 50 mV/s.
(a) Specific capacity and (b) specific capacitance calculated through CV.
a–d GCD graphs for Ag2S, MoS2, Ag2S-MoS2 and Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs at various current densities.
(a) Specific capacity and (b) specific capacitance calculated through GCD.
(a) CV curves (b) GCD curves (c-d) are specific capacity from CV and GCD of Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs//AC.
(a–b) Stability measurements, (c) Energy and power density of Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs//AC device.
(a) LSV curve of Ag2S, MoS2, Ag2S-MoS2, Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs, and Pt/C (b) Overpotential, (c) Tafel slope and (d) stability of LSV curve after 1000 cycles of Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs.
Comparison of stability test for Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs//AC device with literature.
Comparison of Ed and Pd for Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs AC device with literature.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots embedded silver sulfide molybdenum disulfide for efficient electrode materials for hybrid supercapacitors and electrocatalytic hydrogen generation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Development of sustainable and renewable energy sources, such as the water electro-reduction to produce hydrogen and various gadgets for storing energy, particularly increased power density and environmentally amiable supercapacitors, have grown necessary for energy security in light of current environmental concerns. Herein, silver sulfide (Ag2S) and molybdenum sulfide (MoS2) doped (10 wt%) with the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been created and investigated for use in electrochemical processes. In alkaline pH, the Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs heterostructure that was designed had a stable and improved HER (η = 98 mV, Tafel slope: 76 mV/dec) for up to 1000 CV cycles. This was due to its effective interface construction. Due to its exceptional storage capabilities, the asymmetric Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs//AC device also demonstrated good cycling stability (with 92.17 % of the capacitance remaining after 5000 cycles), an increased energy density (71 Wh/kg) and power density (850 W/kg) at 2 A/g), and an exceptional high specific capacity (500C/g at mass standardized current thickness of 2 A/g). Ag2S-MoS2 and rGO interact because of charge transfer from the Ag and Mo 3d orbitals to the C 2p orbitals. Because of this trade, the quantity of states per unit for hybrid structures exhibits amplification close to the Fermi level, indicating a rise in the material's conductivity. Additionally, the ternary heterostructure Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs has the largest quantum capacitance, which supports this system's experimentally determined higher charge storage ability. Technological perceptions on the creation of a cost-effective and efficient catalyst for flexible energy storage applications and sustainable hydrogen production are provided by this work.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In response to the escalating issues of energy depletion and environmental contamination, there has been a surge in scientific study focused on energy to develop a dependable next-creation of energy storage and conversion systems. Significant endeavors have been focused on electrochemical capacitors [1,2] and electrocatalysts for water-dividing processes [1–3]. Electrocatalytic water-dividing primarily consists of two half-responses: the hydrogen evolution response (HER) and the oxygen evolution response (OER). Hydrogen is often regarded as among the most enduring and environmentally friendly energy sources due to its remarkable energy density [4,5]. Out of the different methods for generating hydrogen, the HER process is considered the most promising due to its minimal energy and excellent efficiency requirement, as well as its environmentally favourable approach. Catalysts guarantee the efficiency of yield, rate of reaction, and active spots in electrochemical processes. Pt, Rh, and Ir are precious noble metals; PtIrCo, PtCoRh, and PtRh are their alloys [6–8] oxides (RhO2, IrO2 [9] are thought to be the most effective electrocatalysts because they can stimulate the creation of gaseous hydrogen at nearly zero overpotential. These materials can catalyze the production of gaseous hydrogen with minimal overpotential. The main obstacle to the widespread commercialization of the HER is primarily the limited availability and high cost of platinum group elements, which are essential for its practical implementation. Hence, it is crucial to investigate plentiful, inexpensive functional materials with a wide range of pH tolerance and exceptional reaction rates.

Currently, it is equally crucial to focus on developing energy-storage technologies sustainably to store the surplus energy generated effectively. Batteries and ultracapacitors have continuously improved rivals among storage devices. However, because to the slowly moving ions and electrons, the batteries experience resistance decreases, resulting in overheating. Additionally, the movement of particles in battery systems plays a crucial role in developing dendrites during high-power operation, leading to structural instability and posing significant safety risks. Supercapacitors, in contrast, are environmentally benign and offer several benefits, such as increased power density, rapid charging and discharge velocity and continuous cycle stability and lifespan. However, their growth is hindered by the high cost of materials and low energy density. The coordinates are [10,11]. Hence, the development of efficient energy storage materials that are affordable, have simple preparation methods, exhibit excellent thermal stability, and are non-toxic is crucial.

Transition-metal dichalcogenides (TMDCs) were developed as a solution to the difficulties on this front as MX2 (M-metal, X-chalcogen, CoSe2, MnSe2, CoS2, MoSe2, MoS2, Ag2S NiSe2, FeSe2, WSe2, etc.) [12–18] have emerged as the leading candidates. Specifically, metal sulfide have been investigated earlier for HER and OER [19,20], solar cells sensitive to dyes [21], batteries [22], optoelectronic apparatus [23], and so on, primarily because of their earth abundance, multivalent oxidation states, structural variety, and adjustable physicochemical characteristics. Silver sulfide (Ag2S) and molybdenum sulfide (MoS2) Nanostructured materials have substantially lower toxicity and unparalleled catalytic activity. Regrettably, Ag2S-MoS2 electronic conductivity is insufficient for huge scope energy transformation or capacity applications. Consequently, various tactical methods, such as heteroatom-doping, surface designing, carbon nanomaterial coupling, defect engineering, and heterostructure synthesis, have been shown to be effective in adjusting the vehicle proficiency of the electroactive materials [24,25]. For the more advancement in structure and higher conductivity introduced rGO as dopant 10 wt% in to the Ag2S-MoS2 composite.

We have developed and investigated silver sulfide (Ag2S) and molybdenum sulfide (MoS2) doped (10 wt%) with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for electrochemical processes [26]. In alkaline pH, the Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs heterostructure demonstrated stable and enhanced hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) efficiency (η = 98 mV, Tafel slope: 76 mV/dec) across 1000 CV cycles due to effective interface construction. The asymmetric Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs/AC device showcased high energy thickness (71 Wh/kg at 850 W/kg and 2 A/g), exceptional specific capacity (500C/g at mass normalized current density of 2 A/g), and strong cycling stability with 92.17 % capacitance retention after 5000 cycles. The charge transfer from Ag and Mo 3d orbitals to C 2p orbitals in Ag2S-MoS2 and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) enhances the thickness of states close to the Fermi level, improving conductivity. The Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs ternary heterostructure displayed the highest quantum capacitance, aligning with its superior charge storage observed experimentally. This work offers insights into developing cost-effective catalysts for flexible energy storage and sustainable hydrogen production. For the first time, we have introduced Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs heterostructures with rich electrochemical activity, making them effective as bifunctional anode materials for both hydrogen advancement and supercapacitor applications, featuring high conductivity, explicit surface region, and stable-redox dynamic primary arrangement. HER activity and supercapacitive efficiency were compared with existing literature, supported by the Dun method for capacitance analysis, bonding mechanism, quantum capacitance, and electrical properties, affirming the system's charge storage and HER capabilities.

The raw materials used in this research were used without further treatment, including sodium molybdate (Na2MoO4, 98 %), thiourea (CH4N2S, 98 %), ammonia (NH3, 98 %), silver nitrate (AgNO3), sodium sulfide pentahydrate (Na2S·5H2O), potassium hydroxide (KOH) and activated carbon (AC) were acquired from Sigma Aldrich.

The Ag2S nanoparticles were synthesized via a precipitation method utilizing AgNO3 and Na2S·5H2O as precursor materials. A solution of AgNO3 (1.2 g) was prepared by dissolving it in 50 mL of deionized (DI) water. Subsequently, a solution of 0.003 mol Na2S·5H2O was added dropwise to the above solution while subjecting it to a continuous stirrer at room temperature for 1 h, furthermore, the mixture was vigorously agitated for 6 h. Subsequently, the mixture was washed and centrifuged multiple times using ethanol and distilled water. Following the filtration process and subsequent vacuum drying at a temperature of 120 °C, Ag2S was obtained as a black powder.

To synthesize the MoS2 nanocomposite, 0.7 mol of Na2MoO4 and 0.3 mol of CH4N2S were added to a 50 mL. The mixture was then subjected to ultrasonication for approximately 1 h and a temperature range of 50 °C to achieve a uniform suspension. The pH of this combination was subsequently modified to around 7–8 by introducing NH3 solution. Subsequently, the mixture was moved to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave reactor and filled to approximately 80 % of its volume. The mixture was then subjected to a temperature of 320 °C for 24 h. The autoclaves were subsequently removed from the furnace and allowed to cool to ambient temperature to collect the synthesized precipitates. The obtained precipitates were subjected to multiple washes using DI water and ethanol, followed by centrifugation and drying at a temperature of 60 °C for 24 h in a vacuum furnace. These powders were then collected and utilized for property characterization. In addition, a hydrothermal process was employed to synthesize Ag2S-MoS2 composites decorated with GQDs. This synthesis involved the incorporation of equal molar ratios of Ag2S and MoS2 precursors and the introduction of 10 wt% GQDs as dopants (Fig. 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Working electrode fabrication and electrochemical measurements
ce:section.ce:para: In the initial phase, nickel foam was precisely cut into 1 × 1 cm2 sections and underwent a wash with a 1 M HCl solution before substrate deposition. NMP was employed as the processing solvent. Homogeneous slurry was prepared by meticulously combining 80 wt% of the active material, 10 wt% carbon black, and 10 wt% PVDF binder. This blend was vigorously stirred for 8 h to ensure uniformly mixed slurry. Subsequently, this slurry was evenly applied onto a 1 × 1 cm2 nickel foam substrate. The working electrode was then dried at 60 °C prior to the commencement of electrochemical characterizations. A three-electrode assembly was employed for electrochemical measurements, comprising an Hg/HgO electrode as a reference, a platinum wire as a counter electrode, and the fabricated nickel foam electrode as the working electrode. Electrochemical analyses were carried out using a 2 M KOH electrolyte. The performance of active material electrodes was thoroughly evaluated through cyclic voltammetry (CV) at different scan rates, galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) at various current densities, and Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) within the three-electrode assembly setup. Upon testing all samples, it became evident that the material designated as Ag2S-MoS2@GQDs exhibited superior performance.

Consequently, this optimal sample was selected as the electrode for the supercapattery in a two-electrode assembly, with activated carbon serving as the second electrode. A current density of two to ten A/g was maintained. Furthermore, utilizing quick test techniques, the durability of this hybrid apparatus was examined at room temperature. We use the following equation to calculate the specific capacity through CV of the working electrodes, including Ag2S, MoS2, and Ag2S-MoS2@GQDs [27].
(1)
Qs =
1 mv
∫ vi vf
I × Vdv

We used this equation to calculate the specific capacity of GCD testing [27].
(2)
Qs =
I × t
m

The above equation I indicates the current “t” discharge time and m is for active mass. We used the equations to find the energy and power density of Ag2S, MoS2, Ag2S-MoS2 and Ag2S-MoS2@GQDs [28,29].
(3)
E =
Q × Δ V
2 × 3.6
(4)
P =
E × 3600
Δ t
In the above equations, E indicates energy density, Qs is for specific capacity, the term potential is indicated by V, the time is indicated with t, and P indicates power density.

The exploration examines added into the crystal characteristics of several integrated particles, enveloping silver sulfide, molybdenum sulfide, and silver molybdenum sulfide. Applying X-ray diffraction, the evaluation abandons meaningful observations as characterized in Fig. 2a. In particular, discernible peaks at 27.1°, 30.3°, 33.1°, 35.2°, 38.4°, 41.6°, and 44.8° belongs to (012), (111), (−112), (−121), (121), (031), and (023) planes, aligning with JCPDF-089-3840-Ag2S [30,31]. Furthermore the peaks at 14.6°, 34.3°, 40.7°, and 59.2° belong to the corresponding planes (002), (100), (103), and (110), respectively [32]. These heights were closely connected with JCPDF-37-1492-MoS2, thereby approving the existence of MoS2. In addition, the X-ray diffraction methodology was expertly utilized to examine the composition of silver sulfide and molybdenum sulfide. All heights noticed in the amalgam cross section were raised to coincide with nickel sulfide properties. This exhaustive examine clarify on the intricate behaviour of the integrated particles and their crystalline frameworks. The Debye-Scherrer formula was utilized to commutated crystallite size.
(5)
D =
kλ βcosθ

The above formula D shows the crystalline size, λ denotes wavelength and the β is showing full-width half maximum. The crystallized size for Ag2S-MoS2 was 68 nm, Ag2S was 64 nm, and for MoS2 was 60 nm.

UV analysis was executed on Ag2S, MoS2, and their amalgam (AgS2-MoS2) to examine their electronic characteristics and absorbance properties (Fig. 2b). The UV spectra of Ag2S indicated distinct absorption heights at some wavelengths, revealing electronic conversions. For instance, strong heights were distinguished at around 320 nm, showing electron excitation from the valence band to the conduction band. In comparison, MoS2 indicated different absorption heights, with remarkable properties happening at wavelengths like 260 nm and 380 nm, characterizing the particle's specific electronic conversions. Interestingly, the UV spectra of the Ag2S-MoS2 amalgam showed a mixed spectrum that accommodated absorption heights from both the Ag2S and MoS2 substances. The amalgam spectrum has an extensive absorption outline, with heights relating to electronic conversion in Ag2S and MoS2 at various wavelengths. This affiliation of absorption properties from both particles in the amalgam framework sheds light on the coefficient of electronic relations and gives beneficial data about its optical characteristics.

Thermogravimetric assessment (TGA) was utilized to examine the thermal constancy of the Ag2S-MoS2amalgam in contrast to its component elements, silver sulfide (AgS2) and molybdenum disulfide (Fig. 2c). The TGA curves indicated that, whereas Ag2S revealed significant weight loss above 250 °C, showing disintegration, and MoS2 indicated enlightened degradation beginning about 320 °C, the Ag2S-MoS2 amalgam displayed lot of bettered thermal constancy by indicating first weigh loss at 430 °C. It much higher decomposition temperature demonstrated the composite enhanced stability, indicating increased resistance to heat deterioration. Such increased stability is most likely due to synergistic collaboration between Ag2S and MoS2 within the combined structure, the protective impact of one component on the other, the composite unique morphology that promotes its rigidity and the chemical compatibility of Ag2S and MoS2. Overall, our findings highlight the higher thermal stability of the Ag2S-MoS2 combination, highlighting its potential for applications that need enhanced temperature resistance.

SEM was used to carefully examine the surface morphological properties of AgS2, MoS2, and Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs (Fig. 3a–d). The SEM image reveals the formation of a homogeneous, coral-like Ag2S nanostructure film (Fig. 3a). This film exhibits a highly porous three-dimensional (3D) nano-architecture network. Ag2S nanostructure consist of nanocorals with dendritic structure, which is 0.1 to 1 μm in diameter and cross-linked with nanosheets [33]. Fig. 3b shows that MoS2 particles agglomerated together to form coral-like materials with a porous microstructure and MoS2 particles shows various size of the coral like. The MoS2 had coral-like structures with about 0.2–1 μm diameter which collaborated with large size and large number of nanosheets. SEM analysis (Fig. 3c) reveals an increase in both nanosheet and particle size upon Ag2S incorporation into MoS2. Additionally, the image suggests enhanced stacking of the composite material. This observed interconnection between Ag2S and MoS2 structures potentially facilitates improved charge transport, which could benefit electrochemical applications. Incorporation of GQDs into the Ag2S-MoS2 heterostructure enhances the material porosity, resulting in a larger surface area and improved ion diffusion pathways (Fig. 3d). The objective of this study is to examine the synthesis of Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs surface to enhance our comprehension of their unique characteristics and prospective applications in electrochemical contexts.

The valence states of Ag 3d, Mo 3d, S 2p, and C 1s, in the composite (Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs) sample were determined using XPS, as illustrated in Fig. 4a–e. The XPS spectra of Ag revealed two distinct peaks separated by almost 7.6 eV gap. The peaks corresponding to Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2 appeared at 366.7 eV and 374.3 eV, respectively (Fig. 4a) both of which match with Ag + ions [27]. Fig. 4b presents the XPS spectrum of the Mo 3d binding energy region for the as-prepared MoS2 composite. The spectrum exhibits two distinct peaks, which, after curve fitting, were attributed to the characteristic spin–orbit doublet of Mo 3d levels are 227.5 eV and 234.2 eV, indicating Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2 [34]. The XPS spectra for S 2p were indicated in Fig. 4c. Peaks at 161.8 and 164.2 eV appeared, which belong to S2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 [27]. C 1s were due to the inclusion of GQDs into the Ag2S-MoS2 heterostructure. The C 1s spectrum shows distinct peaks for various carbon species, including sp2 carbon (C=C) bonds around 283.3 eV and (C=O) bonds around 287.5 eV (Fig. 4d) [35]. The XPS spectra of Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs composite typically represents the quantity of electrons measured at a particular binding energy (Fig. 4e).

The EIS were performed to evaluate the internal resistance between the electrolyte and electrode of the AgS2-MoS2 and Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs. Approaching a frequency range from 0.1 to 100 kHz, the EIS determinations aspired to examine several frameworks such as capacitive nature, resistance to charge transfer and equivalent series resistance. The result EIS graphs displayed a property framework, originally declaring as a small semicircle that constantly transitioned into a direct division. The Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) graph is semicircular (semicircle at higher frequencies is typical of charge transfer processes in electrochemical systems) with a slightly curved tail at low frequencies, which provides insight into the electrochemical properties of the materials analysis of the EIS measurements facilitated the determination of the resistance at the electrolyte-electrode interface, or equivalent series resistance, or ESR. The Nyquist graph's high-frequency region was used for ESR determination, resulting in values of 1.32 and 1.27 Ω for Ag2S-MoS2 and Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs, respectively, as illustrated in Fig. 5a of the study. The sample exhibiting higher performance was then chosen for more research into its supercapattery and HER based on performance on these EIS data. The inset shows an equivalent circuit model used to interpret the EIS data. It includes components like resistors (Rs for solution resistance, Rct for charge transfer resistance) and capacitors (CPE, which likely represents constant phase elements to model double-layer capacitance behaviour). This equivalent circuit provides a theoretical framework to fit the impedance data and extract values for resistances and capacitances.

BET an isotherm was used to examine the surface area (SA) and porosity properties of Ag2S-MoS2 and Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs (Fig. 5b–c). Within the pressure range of 0 to 1.0 P/Po, the BET isotherm displayed IV-type behaviour, with a N2 hysteresis loop present, indicative of the materials' mesoporous structure, as shown in Fig. 5b. The surface areas (SA) for Ag2S-MoS2 and Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs were measured at 72 m2/g and 81 m2/g, respectively. Notably, the larger SA of the AgS2-MoS2/GQDs composite suggested increased accessibility of electrochemical reaction contact points, contributing to its enhanced electrochemical performance. Ag2S-MoS2 exhibited a pore size of 20.13 nm, while Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs displayed a slightly larger pore size of 22.15 nm. The increased pore size of the Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs composite facilitates more efficient ion input throughout the charging/discharging procedure, enhancing the accessibility and capability of ions for storage. Consequently, Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs surpass Ag2S-MoS2 in terms of overall storage performance.

We utilized three-electrode assembly kits for the electrochemical performance of all fabricated electrodes: the reference electrode was Hg/HgO, and the counter electrode was platinum wire. The testing window selected for CV is (0 V–0.7 V) in 2 M KOH electrolyte at low scan rate as 3 mV/s to a high scan rate of 50 mV/s [36]. CV analysis can help us examine the inherent qualities of the synthesized materials (Fig. 6a–d). CV measurements define the charge storage mechanism in the materials. The CV curves show the apparent oxidation and reduction trends and this faradic behaviour means that tested materials belong to the battery grade family. We can observe an increase in doped material anodic and cathodic current in all the CV. For example, the anodic current of a single material fabricated electrode like Ag2S, MoS2, is lower than the composite material Ag2S-MoS2. The current also increased as we doped the composited material with 10 wt% GQDs. As the current increased, the electrode had a more specific capacity.

The processes governed by diffusion adhered to Randles-Sevcik's mathematical framework for predicting the peak current (Fig. S1a–d). The electroactive effective area of fabricated electrodes (Fig. 6a–d) are calculated by using Randles–Sevcik equation:
(6)
i p
= 0.4463 nF
nFD RT
AC
V

In Eq. (6), the symbol “ip” represents the peak current, “F” stands for the Faraday constant, “n” denotes the number of electrons, “T” signifies the temperature in Kelvin, “A” refers to the surface area, “C” represents the bulk concentration, and “D” represents the diffusion constant.

Through the Randles–Sevcik equation we have to calculate the electrochemical surface area (ECSA) from the CV curves. The calculated ECSA are 0.0136, 0.067, 0.102 and 0.145 cm2 of the Ag2S, MoS2, Ag2S-MoS2 and Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs composite. Maximum positive and negative currents were computed for Ag2S, MoS2, Ag2S-MoS2 and Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs, depicted in Fig. S1a–c. The linear correlation between the anodic and cathodic maxima confirms the diffusion-controlled storage system. Furthermore, determining the R2 value derived from these plots' slope, which was close to 1, validates the battery-like behaviour of Ag2S, MoS2, Ag2S-MoS2 and Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs. Consequently, the diffusion coefficient for Ag2S, MoS2, Ag2S-MoS2 and Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs plotting peak currents versus the square root approximates the scanning speed. As evident from these figures, the peak currents consistently exhibit proportionality to the square root of the scan rate, showing that the charge storage system is entirely diffusion-controlled. A linear relationship between the scan rate's logarithm and peak voltage is demonstrated in Fig. S1d, providing further support for the diffusion-controlled charge storage system.

All the electrodes' Qs are calculated using Eq. (1) through CV. The specific capacity obtained for Ag2S is 813C/g, MoS2 is 1310C/g, Ag2S-MoS2 has 1547C/g and the doped (Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs) electrode capacity is 1799C/g (Fig. 7a). By using the equation, Csp =
Q
m Δ V
, the specific capacitance calculated via equation is as follows Ag2S show a specific capacitance is 1353 F/g, MoS2 2155 F/g, Ag2S-MoS2 2578 F/g and Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs showed 3019 F/g (Fig. 7b). The combination of Ag2S with MoS2 increased the conductivity and surface area, but with the doping of GQDs both these two properties were enhanced. Maximum Surface area and conductivity are two crucial components of electrochemical measurements [38]. Area increased at a high scan rate due to reduced charge/discharge time [32]. The significant redox peaks, mostly from the interaction of the electrode material with OH−1 ions, confirm the battery-type character of the produced materials [37]. Based on the CV results, it may be inferred that a binary GQDs doped composite is higher than all other three fabricated electrodes.

The electrochemical performance of Ag2S, MoS2, Ag2S-MoS2 and Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs is also examined in a constant potential window at different current densities utilizing GCD testing (Fig. 8a–d). Every measurement was done using an electrolyte of 2 M KOH. The Charge/discharge time of Ag2S and MoS2 are almost near to each other, but the composite time increased with a value of 825 s at 2 A/g, and the GQDs doped electrode time is nearly doubled as compared to simple composite which is 1775 s at 2 A/g. All the GCD curves showed the battery-grade material behaviour, proving that all these tested electrodes belong to battery-grade family materials [39]. The results reveal the redox response of every synthesized material, which demonstrates that all of the GCD curves are irregular and deviate from the triangle trend. The irregular response of GCD curves demonstrates redox processes at the electrode surface. Furthermore, the irregular response of GCD curves supports our conclusion from CV data for battery-grade electrode materials. Raising the current densities reduces the charge/discharge time length, but the form of the curves remains unaltered, suggesting the stability of the produced materials.

Because there is more time to engage with the electrode material, there is a reduction in discharge time as current densities rise. Consequently, specific capacity likewise declines. With Eq. (2), the specific capacity of electrodes Ag2S, MoS2, Ag2S-MoS2 and Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs were calculated which are as follows 1088C/g, 1288C/g, 1960C/g and 3035C/g (Fig. 9a). The specific capacitance of all the electrodes was also calculated using equation Csp =
I m
∇ t
∇ V
. The specific capacitance obtained from GCD curves for Ag2S, MoS2, Ag2S-MoS2 and Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs are as Ag2S shows 1847F/g, MoS2 2227 F/g, Ag2S-MoS2 has 3287 F/g and Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs showed 5108 F/g (Fig. 9b). The highest possible specific capacity of 1797C/g at 3 mV/s and an increased rate capacity is achieved for the doped binary composite Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs. Due to limited contact time, specific capacity gradually declines as scan rates rise. The characterizations through the CV and GCD measurements Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs show the best results, so this electrode was further preferred for the fabrication of supercapattery devices.

To compose an asymmetric apparatus, we engaged Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs//AC as the anode and AC as the cathode. A semipermeable membrane (WHATMAN paper) was utilized in a hybrid apparatus to anticipate electron movement between the anode and cathode. We adjusted a 2 M KOH solution for the electrolytic result in DI water. In a three-electrode adjustment, we first settle capacitive and battery electrodes. The implied window for AC was secure at −1 to 0 V, while the working possibility for Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs//AC was recollected at 0–0.6 V. The OP in a two-electrode adjustment was larger and affirmed at 0–1.6 V. In Fig. 10a the perpetual CV graphs for the Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs//AC in a two-cell adjustment at several scanning speeds of 3 to 100 mV/s are denoted. These angles have a resistive (rectangular) form at lower potential and a battery-type (faradic peaks) at the higher potential. Even at higher scans, the Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs//AC asymmetric apparatus measure possibility and adherence were alike powerful. The GCD measurements are shown in Fig. 10b. The approximately triangular forms of GCD angles with a small horizontal section promoted our aim of supercapattery conclusion. The triangular shape indicates the augmentations from EDLC, and little elevations indicate the augmentation from the battery. The specific capacity calculated through CV was 325C/g and through GCD was 503C/g (Fig. 10c–d).

Fig. 11a–b shows the constancy of this supercapattery (Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs//AC) by liberating it to 5000 continuous charging-discharging cycles. Ag2S-MoS2 doped with 10 % GQDs displayed a charge efficiency of 93.16 % and a discharge efficiency of 98.42 % after undergoing 5000 cycles, indicating its remarkable reversibility. This hybrid apparatus's possibility maintenance was 92.17 %, and columbic efficiency was 98.15 % after the finalization of 5000 cycles. Table 1 shows a comparison of stability performance. Through Eqs. (3)–(4) are used to calculate the energy and power densities, which are 71 W h k/g and 850 W k/g. As depicted graphically in Fig. 11c. The correlation of energy and power density with the formerly reputed values is represented in Table 2 . As a result of our estimations, we consider that applying an Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs//AC has appreciable potential for future energy-storing methods.

Employing Dunn's model across various scan rates provided valuable perspectives on the interplay between capacitive and diffusive mechanisms within the Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs//AC supercapattery (Fig. S2) [45].
(7)
i
V
=
K 1
V
1 / 2
+
K 2
V

In Eq. (7),
K 1
V
1 / 2
and
K 2
V represents the capacitive and diffusive participation. The results revealed a notable disparity in the impact of capacitive and diffusive mechanisms. Specifically, at a scan rate of 3 mV/s, capacitive involvement was identified at 12.3 % (Fig. S2a). This suggests that a substantial portion of charge storage during electrochemical operations stems from the faradic behaviour of the supercapattery. At this relatively low scan rate, redox reactions dominate the storage mechanism, facilitated by the reversible integration of ions into the electrode material's lattice structure, enabling higher charge storage capacity. However, this process often leads to slower ion diffusion but yields a higher specific capacity at the expense of lower power density. Conversely, at a higher scan rate of 100 mV/s, capacitive involvement significantly increased to 43.93 %. Capacitive behaviour takes precedence in charge storage compared to lower scan rates. This phenomenon is attributed to the rapid development of the electrical double layer and ions' rapid adsorption and desorption at the electrode-electrolyte interface. Capacitive charge storage primarily occurs at the surface level, offering superior power densities but lower energy densities. The accelerated kinetics associated with the capacitive charge storage mechanism may elucidate the observed trend of heightened capacitive involvement at higher scan rates. The Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs offered strong prospects for use in devices that store energy in the future (Fig. S2b).

The fabricated electrocatalytic activity is tested in an electrolyte containing 2 M KOH using a three-electrode setup. All electrochemical measurements are conducted with identical concentrations of nanocatalysts applied to a NF substrate [46]. The nanocatalysts Ag2S, MoS2, Ag2S-MoS2, Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs, and Pt/C (used as a reference) are shown in Fig. 12a along with their LSV (linear sweep voltammetry) curves. Regarding electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution, the Pt/C catalyst performs the best out of all the others. More advantageous than the overpotentials of Ag2S (239 mV), MoS2 (140 mV), and Ag2S-MoS2 (108 mV) is the Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs catalyst, which exhibits an overpotential of about 98 mV at 10 mV/s (Fig. 12b). Ag2S-MoS2 catalytically active site count and HER performance are increased when GQDs are added. The catalysts HER kinetics can be approximated using their matching Tafel graphs. The Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs nanocatalyst has a Tafel slope of 76 mV/dec, which is significantly lower than that of Ag2S (120 mV/dec), MoS2 (111 mV/dec), Ag2S-MoS2 (87 mV/dec), and Pt/C (64 mV/dec). The nano catalyst has good kinetic properties and follows the Volmer-Heyrovsky process, as indicated by the modest Tafel slope of Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs (Fig. 12c). The HER outcomes of the Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs revealed that the presence of a porous configuration facilitates extensive surface interaction and promotes the formation of a metallic phase. ZIF-8 enhances the swift transfer of ions and electrons between the electrolyte and electrocatalyst composites, elevating HER performance. The rapid charge transfer and transit mechanism played a significant role in the high HER efficiency demonstrated by Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs as evidenced by the electrochemical impedance spectrum (Fig. 12d). The fabricated electrodes for the HER exhibited minimal internal resistance and excellent conductivity, the increase in internal electrode resistances can be attributed to the gradual deterioration of either the electrode material or the electrolyte after HER measurements (Fig. 12e).

In this research work, Ag2S-MoS2 was doped with GQDs as electrode material for supercapattery and HER applications. Furthermore, the Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs heterointerface's special structure optimizes and enhances the proton chemisorption and water dissociation processes, resulting in better HER activity. The fabricated real gadget with a limited time of operation of 1.6 V achieves a high energy density of 71 Wh/kg at a power density of 850 W/kg of the Ag2S-MoS2/GQDs//AC. The apparatus produced extended, steady cycling (retention of capacitance ∼99.17 %), possessing superior Coulombic efficiency (98.15 %). The capacitive contribution's predominance was 43.93 % in a sweep rate of 100 mV/s. The enhancement observed in GQD heterostructures could be attributed to increased conductivity and electrocatalytic surface region, mitigated by charge move from Ag2S-MoS2 to carbon allotropes. The best consequences of the stable heterostructures displayed above demonstrate the possibility to fabricate an eminent HER electrocatalyst and a reasonable positive terminal for unbalanced supercapacitor applications.h

Tasawar Abbas: Writing – original draft. Ehtisham Umar: Writing – review & editing. Haseebul Hassan: Resources. M. Waqas Iqbal: Supervision. Hameed Ullah: Software, Data curation. Sherdil Khan: Methodology. Saikh Mohammad: Funding acquisition. Nouf H. Alotaibi: Writing – original draft, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Resources, Conceptualization, Software, Data curation. N.A. Ismayilova: Validation. Akbar Mohammad: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation. Yazen M. Alawaideh: Formal analysis.
